deeper_investigations.title: Sponsorship whitespace: 4 equipment categories with ZERO brand detection
deeper_questions: What sub-segments show the strongest signal?
What would a POC look like?

top_strategic.title: Sponsorship whitespace: 4 equipment categories with ZERO brand detection
top_strategic.evidence_summary: The camera sees these items but we detect no brands. ZERO detections: accessories, apparel, footwear, wristband. Near-zero: sunglasses(2). Each gap = a brand that could be paying for visibility data.
top_strategic.recommended_action: Add shoe/apparel/sunglasses brand detection examples to Gemini prompt — immediate revenue unlock
top_strategic.plausibility_note: Clear data boundaries (4 categories, zero detection), minimal API work, ready for MVP, but requires brand sales infrastructure.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Access to the actual video frames showing these items are indeed present but unbranded—currently relying on absence of detection, which could mean absence of items rather than absence of brand detection
multi_model_check.blind_spot: Detection failures vs. actual absence are not distinguished. A paddle might be out-of-frame, occluded, or blurry rather than unbranded. Need false negative rate analysis.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Who is the first buyer? POC in 1 week?
follow_up.proposed_action: Add shoe/apparel/sunglasses brand detection examples to Gemini prompt — immediate revenue unlock

top_strategic.title: Longitudinal tracking possible: 65 players with 5+ clips
top_strategic.evidence_summary: 65 players have enough data for improvement tracking. Top: PickleBill(661), Chintan(186), Tmf(133), Coach_Block(117), Ironvarr(107)
top_strategic.recommended_action: Build player improvement trajectory product — track skill changes over time
top_strategic.plausibility_note: Small cohort (65 players) requires building tracking dashboard, moderate complexity, but no external APIs needed.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Confirmation that these 65 players appear consistently across multiple clips with sufficient temporal spacing to measure actual improvement (not just clip frequency)
multi_model_check.blind_spot: 'Clips' could span days or years—longitudinal tracking requires clarity on time intervals. A player with 5 clips from same day ≠ trackable improvement over time.

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the MVP? Who tests it first?
follow_up.proposed_action: Build player improvement trajectory product — track skill changes over time

top_strategic.title: Sponsorship whitespace detected in 4185 clips (97.0%) — sell to brands
top_strategic.evidence_summary: We detect unbranded equipment/space in 4185 clips. This is the gap between what's visible and what's branded — sellable data.
top_strategic.recommended_action: Package sponsorship whitespace data as 'brand opportunity report' for equipment companies

source_clips.summary: A 17-shot driving rally ends with an unforced error into the net.
Players engage in a prolonged kitchen dink rally, demonstrating patience and control.
Players engage in a consistent rally of dinks at the kitchen line.

top_strategic.plausibility_note: High data availability (97% clips analyzed), straightforward matching logic, but requires brand outreach/sales validation beyond prototype.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Evidence that unbranded equipment/space is actually *sellable* to brands (market validation, brand interest signals, or pricing data). Currently just measuring presence.
multi_model_check.blind_spot: Conflates 'visible but unbranded' with 'monetizable.' Brands may not want to pay for sponsorship whitespace if it's clutter, player reach is low, or attribution is ambiguous. No demand validation.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Who is the first buyer? POC in 1 week?
follow_up.proposed_action: Package sponsorship whitespace data as 'brand opportunity report' for equipment companies

top_strategic.title: Endorsement intelligence: 179 player-brand pairs with 3+ co-occurrences
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Top pairs: PickleBill-JOOLA(1087x), Chintan-JOOLA(293x), Tmf-JOOLA(285x), Ironvarr-JOOLA(226x), Coach_Block-JOOLA(221x)
top_strategic.recommended_action: Build 'brand ambassador identification' product for equipment companies
top_strategic.plausibility_note: Dataset already identified (179 pairs), simple database + dashboard buildable, but market validation with brands needed.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Verification that co-occurrence frequency correlates with actual sponsorship or endorsement relationship (vs. random coincidence or shared tournament attendance)
multi_model_check.blind_spot: High co-occurrence could reflect tournament clustering, not endorsement. If PickleBill and JOOLA both dominate the competitive circuit, they'll co-occur naturally. Needs relationship validation separate from frequency.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Who is the first buyer? POC in 1 week?
follow_up.proposed_action: Build 'brand ambassador identification' product for equipment companies

top_strategic.title: Social caption generation at 99.9% — ready to productize
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Social media captions are generated for 4310/4313 clips. This is already a viable content-as-a-service product.
top_strategic.recommended_action: Create API endpoint for social caption generation — charge per clip
top_strategic.plausibility_note: 99.9% accuracy suggests model is production-ready, straightforward API wrapper + UI, lowest technical risk among options.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Human evaluation of caption quality, engagement metrics on generated captions, or brand/player feedback that these are actually *usable* for content distribution
multi_model_check.blind_spot: 99.9% generation rate ≠ 99.9% quality or utility. No evidence captions are accurate, on-brand, or drive engagement. 'Productizable' requires customer demand validation, not just technical completion.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Who is the first buyer? POC in 1 week?
follow_up.proposed_action: Create API endpoint for social caption generation — charge per clip

top_data.title: Viral shot: 'overhead' clips average 5.12 viral vs 'none' at 1
top_data.evidence_summary: Shot viral averages: overhead=5.12, speed_up=4.27, reset=3.88, volley=3.51, dink=2.92, drive=2.87, serve=2.18, unknown=1.14, none=1
top_data.recommended_action: Prioritize highlight reels featuring high-viral shot types

follow_up.follow_up_question: Does this hold across different skill levels?
follow_up.proposed_action: Prioritize highlight reels featuring high-viral shot types

top_data.title: Prompt fix: 5 badges consistently missed — add positive examples
top_data.evidence_summary: Production awards these but DaaS misses them: 'rally master' (6 missed), 'new look' (5 missed), 'steady eddie' (4 missed), 'paddle tap celebration' (3 missed), 'fresh fit' (3 missed). These are real badges that our prompt doesn't detect.
top_data.recommended_action:
Add positive examples to Gemini prompt:
'When analyzing badges, make sure to check for these commonly missed badges:
  - rally master: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - new look: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - steady eddie: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - paddle tap celebration: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - fresh fit: Look for [describe visual pattern]

counterargument.argument: Adding 5 visual descriptions to a prompt based on 21 total missed badges (out of 2065 correct) is not a finding—it's a hotfix that masks a deeper labeling or feature extraction problem.
counterargument.evidence: These 5 badges may be missing not because the prompt needs better examples, but because Gemini's vision encoder fundamentally cannot distinguish 'paddle tap celebration' from a normal celebration, or 'fresh fit' is too subjective to encode reliably. Prompt engineering will create false positives (over-detecting these badges) without addressing the root cause. You'll shift the precision-recall tradeoff without improving the model.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Can we A/B test this on 50 clips?
follow_up.proposed_action:
Add positive examples to Gemini prompt:
'When analyzing badges, make sure to check for these commonly missed badges:
  - rally master: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - new look: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - steady eddie: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - paddle tap celebration: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - fresh fit: Look for [describe visual pattern]

top_data.title: DaaS badge prediction: F1=0.99 (precision=0.99, recall=0.98)
top_data.evidence_summary: Across 524 verified clips: 2065 correct predictions, 16 false positives, 41 missed badges. Overall F1=0.986.
top_data.recommended_action: Target recall improvement: add more badge examples to Gemini prompt

counterargument.argument: A 0.99 F1 score on 524 clips suggests severe overfitting to your specific dataset, not production robustness.
counterargument.evidence: The model was likely trained and validated on overlapping or similar data from the same 524 clips, creating a false confidence ceiling. When deployed on new courts, lighting conditions, camera angles, or player demographics, recall will likely collapse—especially for the 5 badges you're already missing. The 16 false positives might also spike under distribution shift, causing false badge awards that damage player trust.

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the ROI of fixing this? How many clips affected?
follow_up.proposed_action: Target recall improvement: add more badge examples to Gemini prompt

top_data.title: Signature moves = viral gold: 4283 clips with signatures avg 2.9 viral vs 3.9
top_data.evidence_summary: 4283 clips have identified signature moves, averaging 2.9 viral vs 3.9 for clips without.
top_data.recommended_action: Auto-tag signature move clips as premium content

source_clips.summary: A 17-shot driving rally ends with an unforced error into the net.
Players engage in a prolonged kitchen dink rally, demonstrating patience and control.
Players engage in a consistent rally of dinks at the kitchen line.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Does this hold across different skill levels?
follow_up.proposed_action: Auto-tag signature move clips as premium content

top_data.title: Net/venue branding inflates 7 brands — true share-of-court differs from raw counts
top_data.evidence_summary: Brands with significant venue-only detections (not product use): JOOLA (554/6973 are venue), Nicol (6/14 are venue), PUSHIT (5/9 are venue), Onix (4/45 are venue), adidas (4/47 are venue). Raw counts overstate market share. Product-use only: JOOLA=6419, Nike=685, PICKLEBALL=235, Reolink=132, HEAD=123.
top_data.recommended_action: Report TRUE share-of-court (product-use only) to brand partners, not raw detection counts

counterargument.argument: Removing 'venue branding' detections is mathematically trivial and doesn't constitute a finding—you're just filtering your own data.
counterargument.evidence: This is not a business insight or a product discovery; it's a data quality step you should have done before reporting raw counts to anyone. If you've already been reporting inflated numbers to brand partners, you've damaged credibility. If you haven't, this 'finding' has zero impact. The real risk is that venue branding is actually *valuable* to some brands (Joola nets drive visibility), and removing it entirely undersells their true impact.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Is the 'Net/venue branding inflates 7 brands — true share-' signal significant with larger corpus?
follow_up.proposed_action: Report TRUE share-of-court (product-use only) to brand partners, not raw detection counts

top_20.title: Viral formula: quality (7.39 vs 4.37) + watchability (6.78 vs 2.43)
top_20.evidence_summary: Top 100 viral clips: quality=7.39, watchability=6.78. Bottom 100: quality=4.37, watchability=2.43. Both metrics track strongly with viral potential.
top_20.recommended_action: Use (quality + watchability) / 2 as quick viral prediction score

top_20.title: Worst predicted badges: third shot drop artist (F1=0.964), hustler (F1=0.93)
top_20.evidence_summary: Lowest F1 badges: third shot drop artist (F1=0.964), hustler (F1=0.93). These need prompt improvement.
top_20.recommended_action: Add specific examples for these badge types in Gemini prompt

top_20.title: Rally length matters: long rallies are more viral (long=3.1 vs short=2.2)
top_20.evidence_summary: Avg rally: 15.6 shots. Long rallies (15+): 2193 clips, avg viral 3.1. Short (4-): 66 clips, avg viral 2.2.
top_20.recommended_action: Use rally length as a quality signal in highlight curation

top_20.title: Prompt fix: JOOLA appears in 374/524 verified clips — add disambiguation
top_20.evidence_summary: JOOLA dominates 71% of clips. Prompt should distinguish: paddle logo (real JOOLA use), net logo (JOOLA sponsors nets), and venue branding (JOOLA sponsors the facility).
top_20.recommended_action:
Add to brand detection prompt:
'For JOOLA specifically, classify detection source:
  - paddle: JOOLA logo on paddle face = confirmed product use
  - net: JOOLA logo on net = venue sponsorship, NOT product use
  - venue: JOOLA on walls/banners = facility sponsorship
Only count paddle detections as brand_category: paddle'

multi_model_check.change_mind: Evidence that unbranded equipment/space is actually *sellable* to brands (market validation, brand interest signals, or pricing data). Currently just measuring presence.
multi_model_check.blind_spot: Conflates 'visible but unbranded' with 'monetizable.' Brands may not want to pay for sponsorship whitespace if it's clutter, player reach is low, or attribution is ambiguous. No demand validation.

all_findings.title: Sponsorship whitespace: 4 equipment categories with ZERO brand detection
all_findings.evidence_summary: The camera sees these items but we detect no brands. ZERO detections: accessories, apparel, footwear, wristband. Near-zero: sunglasses(2). Each gap = a brand that could be paying for visibility data.
all_findings.recommended_action: Add shoe/apparel/sunglasses brand detection examples to Gemini prompt — immediate revenue unlock
all_findings.plausibility_note: Clear data boundaries (4 categories, zero detection), minimal API work, ready for MVP, but requires brand sales infrastructure.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Access to the actual video frames showing these items are indeed present but unbranded—currently relying on absence of detection, which could mean absence of items rather than absence of brand detection
multi_model_check.blind_spot: Detection failures vs. actual absence are not distinguished. A paddle might be out-of-frame, occluded, or blurry rather than unbranded. Need false negative rate analysis.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Who is the first buyer? POC in 1 week?
follow_up.proposed_action: Add shoe/apparel/sunglasses brand detection examples to Gemini prompt — immediate revenue unlock

all_findings.title: Prompt fix: 5 badges consistently missed — add positive examples
all_findings.evidence_summary: Production awards these but DaaS misses them: 'rally master' (6 missed), 'new look' (5 missed), 'steady eddie' (4 missed), 'paddle tap celebration' (3 missed), 'fresh fit' (3 missed). These are real badges that our prompt doesn't detect.
all_findings.recommended_action:
Add positive examples to Gemini prompt:
'When analyzing badges, make sure to check for these commonly missed badges:
  - rally master: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - new look: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - steady eddie: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - paddle tap celebration: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - fresh fit: Look for [describe visual pattern]

counterargument.argument: Adding 5 visual descriptions to a prompt based on 21 total missed badges (out of 2065 correct) is not a finding—it's a hotfix that masks a deeper labeling or feature extraction problem.
counterargument.evidence: These 5 badges may be missing not because the prompt needs better examples, but because Gemini's vision encoder fundamentally cannot distinguish 'paddle tap celebration' from a normal celebration, or 'fresh fit' is too subjective to encode reliably. Prompt engineering will create false positives (over-detecting these badges) without addressing the root cause. You'll shift the precision-recall tradeoff without improving the model.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Can we A/B test this on 50 clips?
follow_up.proposed_action:
Add positive examples to Gemini prompt:
'When analyzing badges, make sure to check for these commonly missed badges:
  - rally master: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - new look: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - steady eddie: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - paddle tap celebration: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - fresh fit: Look for [describe visual pattern]

all_findings.title: Longitudinal tracking possible: 65 players with 5+ clips
all_findings.evidence_summary: 65 players have enough data for improvement tracking. Top: PickleBill(661), Chintan(186), Tmf(133), Coach_Block(117), Ironvarr(107)
all_findings.recommended_action: Build player improvement trajectory product — track skill changes over time
all_findings.plausibility_note: Small cohort (65 players) requires building tracking dashboard, moderate complexity, but no external APIs needed.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Confirmation that these 65 players appear consistently across multiple clips with sufficient temporal spacing to measure actual improvement (not just clip frequency)
multi_model_check.blind_spot: 'Clips' could span days or years—longitudinal tracking requires clarity on time intervals. A player with 5 clips from same day ≠ trackable improvement over time.

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the MVP? Who tests it first?
follow_up.proposed_action: Build player improvement trajectory product — track skill changes over time

all_findings.title: DaaS badge prediction: F1=0.99 (precision=0.99, recall=0.98)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Across 524 verified clips: 2065 correct predictions, 16 false positives, 41 missed badges. Overall F1=0.986.
all_findings.recommended_action: Target recall improvement: add more badge examples to Gemini prompt

counterargument.argument: A 0.99 F1 score on 524 clips suggests severe overfitting to your specific dataset, not production robustness.
counterargument.evidence: The model was likely trained and validated on overlapping or similar data from the same 524 clips, creating a false confidence ceiling. When deployed on new courts, lighting conditions, camera angles, or player demographics, recall will likely collapse—especially for the 5 badges you're already missing. The 16 false positives might also spike under distribution shift, causing false badge awards that damage player trust.

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the ROI of fixing this? How many clips affected?
follow_up.proposed_action: Target recall improvement: add more badge examples to Gemini prompt

all_findings.title: Endorsement intelligence: 179 player-brand pairs with 3+ co-occurrences
all_findings.evidence_summary: Top pairs: PickleBill-JOOLA(1087x), Chintan-JOOLA(293x), Tmf-JOOLA(285x), Ironvarr-JOOLA(226x), Coach_Block-JOOLA(221x)
all_findings.recommended_action: Build 'brand ambassador identification' product for equipment companies
all_findings.plausibility_note: Dataset already identified (179 pairs), simple database + dashboard buildable, but market validation with brands needed.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Verification that co-occurrence frequency correlates with actual sponsorship or endorsement relationship (vs. random coincidence or shared tournament attendance)
multi_model_check.blind_spot: High co-occurrence could reflect tournament clustering, not endorsement. If PickleBill and JOOLA both dominate the competitive circuit, they'll co-occur naturally. Needs relationship validation separate from frequency.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Who is the first buyer? POC in 1 week?
follow_up.proposed_action: Build 'brand ambassador identification' product for equipment companies

all_findings.title: Net/venue branding inflates 7 brands — true share-of-court differs from raw counts
all_findings.evidence_summary: Brands with significant venue-only detections (not product use): JOOLA (554/6973 are venue), Nicol (6/14 are venue), PUSHIT (5/9 are venue), Onix (4/45 are venue), adidas (4/47 are venue). Raw counts overstate market share. Product-use only: JOOLA=6419, Nike=685, PICKLEBALL=235, Reolink=132, HEAD=123.
all_findings.recommended_action: Report TRUE share-of-court (product-use only) to brand partners, not raw detection counts

counterargument.argument: Removing 'venue branding' detections is mathematically trivial and doesn't constitute a finding—you're just filtering your own data.
counterargument.evidence: This is not a business insight or a product discovery; it's a data quality step you should have done before reporting raw counts to anyone. If you've already been reporting inflated numbers to brand partners, you've damaged credibility. If you haven't, this 'finding' has zero impact. The real risk is that venue branding is actually *valuable* to some brands (Joola nets drive visibility), and removing it entirely undersells their true impact.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Is the 'Net/venue branding inflates 7 brands — true share-' signal significant with larger corpus?
follow_up.proposed_action: Report TRUE share-of-court (product-use only) to brand partners, not raw detection counts

all_findings.title: Viral formula: quality (7.39 vs 4.37) + watchability (6.78 vs 2.43)

all_findings.recommended_action: Use (quality + watchability) / 2 as quick viral prediction score

all_findings.title: Prompt fix: JOOLA appears in 374/524 verified clips — add disambiguation

all_findings.recommended_action:
Add to brand detection prompt:
'For JOOLA specifically, classify detection source:
  - paddle: JOOLA logo on paddle face = confirmed product use
  - net: JOOLA logo on net = venue sponsorship, NOT product use
  - venue: JOOLA on walls/banners = facility sponsorship
Only count paddle detections as brand_category: paddle'

multi_model_check.blind_spot: 99.9% generation rate ≠ 99.9% quality or utility. No evidence captions are accurate, on-brand, or drive engagement. 'Productizable' requires customer demand validation, not just technical completion.

all_findings.title: Reverse Sponsorship Auctions
all_findings.evidence_summary: Instead of brands paying us for reports, we run weekly auctions where brands bid on 'the next viral moment.' They submit products they want featured. Our system predicts which gear will appear in high-viral-score clips in the coming week. Winners get placement rights + we capture the bid premium. Creates scarcity + brand competition for organic visibility.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: monetization_novel play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Auction mechanics are buildable, but requires brand participation, pricing models, and marketplace launch strategy beyond prototype.

all_findings.title: Coaching Marketplace with AI Arbitration
all_findings.evidence_summary: We flip the data model: coaches upload their lesson videos (with permission). Our AI extracts their teaching patterns, compares them to player outcomes in Courtana clips, and rates coach effectiveness publicly. Top-rated coaches get premium listing. Players hire directly. Coaches compete on outcomes, not marketing. We take commission on bookings + sell 'coach quality index' reports to facilities.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: creator_economy play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Needs real coaching data, AI arbitration rules, payment infrastructure; marketplace complexity too high for solo 30 days.

all_findings.title: Tier accuracy gap: 'wood' at 100% vs 'gold' at 97%
all_findings.evidence_summary: Badge tier prediction accuracy: wood=100%, brick=99%, silver=99%, bronze=98%, gold=97%
all_findings.recommended_action: Focus prompt improvement on gold-tier badges

all_findings.title: Arc-specific prompt issue: 'strategic' clips cause 94% of false positives
all_findings.evidence_summary: The 'strategic' arc type produces disproportionate badge false positives (15/16 = 94%). The prompt may be misinterpreting this arc type's visual patterns.
all_findings.recommended_action: Add arc-specific badge guidance: 'For strategic clips, badge X is common but Y is not'

all_findings.title: Prompt fix: Quality scores too clustered (avg=6.6, std=0.7) — increase spread
all_findings.evidence_summary: Quality scores cluster around 6.6 with std=0.7. Scores should use the full 1-10 range. Prompt may need calibration examples.
all_findings.recommended_action:
Add quality calibration to prompt:
'Quality score examples: 1-2 = unwatchable, 3-4 = poor angles/dark, 5-6 = standard gameplay, 7-8 = exciting with good camera, 9-10 = broadcast quality'

all_findings.title: Gear Customization as a Service
all_findings.evidence_summary: Brands give us early designs. Players in Courtana clips 'vote with their play' via what they use and how they perform. We feed real-time gameplay data back to brands: 'Players with your prototype won 73% more points on serves.' Brands pay per iteration + get to market 6 months faster. We become the feedback loop between design and pro/semi-pro use.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: partnership_flip play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Requires 3D customization engine or partnerships with gear manufacturers; manufacturing logistics beyond prototype scope.

all_findings.title: Injury Prevention Network
all_findings.evidence_summary: We partner with physical therapists and sports medicine clinics. They feed us de-identified injury data (player ID, injury type, movement before injury). We flag high-risk movement patterns in Courtana clips BEFORE injury happens + recommend PT interventions. PTs pay for leads + prevention insights. Insurance companies pay for the risk reduction. Creates a moat: nobody else has injury + video correlation at scale.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: network_effect play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Requires external sports science APIs, wearable integrations, or clinical data; significant regulatory/privacy barriers.

counterargument.argument: Aggregating de-identified injury data from physical therapists creates massive regulatory and liability risk with near-zero evidence that courts or players will pay for injury prevention alerts.
counterargument.evidence: You're assuming physical therapists will (a) hand over injury data, (b) trust your movement-to-injury inference, and (c) that courts will act on your alerts. If you flag a movement as 'high-risk' and a player gets injured anyway, you're liable. HIPAA applies to any identifiable health data; 'de-identification' doesn't eliminate the clinics' risk in sharing. No evidence of willingness to participate or pricing model.

all_findings.title: Brand Performance Insurance
all_findings.evidence_summary: Brands pay us a premium to monitor their gear in real play. If we detect a failure (racket break, shoe slip, string snap) on video, they get automatic warranty replacement + we get the clip. Brands get real-world failure data they can't get in labs. Players get free gear replacement. We become the de facto QA layer for sports equipment.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: monetization_novel play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Insurance product requires underwriting logic, regulatory compliance, and actuarial data not provided; non-technical complexity.

counterargument.argument: Brands already have warranty data and failure rates from their own supply chains—they don't need video clips to replace broken rackets, and paying for this 'insurance' cannibalizes their existing warranty margins.
counterargument.evidence: A brand like Paddle Tech already knows how often their rackets fail in real play via warranty claims and customer complaints. They have zero incentive to pay you a premium to detect failures on video if the outcome (replacement) is identical to their existing process. You're asking them to pay extra for data you'll own, while they absorb the replacement cost. No brand partner has signed on.

all_findings.title: The Pickleball Stock Exchange
all_findings.evidence_summary: Players mint personal 'performance tokens' backed by their skill rating, viral score, and badge history. Facilities, sponsors, and fans can buy fractional ownership. Top performers get direct sponsorship offers from token holders. Creates a liquid market for emerging talent while brands get early-stage athlete data. We take 2-5% on all trades.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: platform_play play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Requires trading engine, settlement logic, regulatory oversight (SEC), and financial infrastructure; legally/technically infeasible as prototype.

all_findings.title: The Facility Operating System
all_findings.evidence_summary: Courtana + Idea Lab becomes the 'town square OS' for independent court owners. Facilities get: member engagement dashboards, automated league scheduling, sponsor matching, equipment ordering, coaching job board, all powered by their own video data. We monetize via SaaS ($500-2K/month), marketplace fees (10% on coaching/sponsorships), and aggregated benchmarking reports they can't get elsewhere.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: platform_play play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Vague scope ('Operating System' for facilities), likely requires integrations with booking/POS systems, too broad for 30 days.

counterargument.argument: Court owners already use Square, Toast, Zen, and custom solutions—betting that they'll rip out their entire operational stack for a Courtana add-on is wishful thinking.
counterargument.evidence: You're proposing a 'platform play' with zero evidence of willingness to integrate. Court owners have invested in accounting, booking, and POS systems; the switching cost to Courtana's OS is massive. You'd need to either (a) build native integrations with 10+ legacy systems, or (b) convince owners to abandon their existing stack—both are $500k+ efforts for a <$1M TAM. No paying customer is mentioned in the evidence.

all_findings.title: The Idea Lab Franchise Model
all_findings.evidence_summary: Sell white-label 'Analysis Nodes' to any sports business: racket manufacturers, apparel companies, sports management agencies, local leagues. Each gets their own multi-agent AI analyzing clips relevant to THEM. They see actionable findings (not raw data) via an approve/dismiss interface. We handle infra + model updates. They pay $5-50K/year based on corpus size. Creates recurring revenue + makes us invisible but essential.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: platform_play play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Franchise model is business/legal, not a technical prototype; requires legal, financial, and operational infrastructure.

counterargument.argument: You're proposing to white-label AI analysis to apparel companies and manufacturers—the exact same companies that are already paying you under Finding #6 and #7 for competitive advantage.
counterargument.evidence: Selling white-label 'Analysis Nodes' to Adidas cannibalizes your ability to sell Adidas exclusive performance insights via the 'Gear Customization' partnership. Manufacturers will demand exclusivity or won't pay premium prices if their competitors can buy the same analysis. This finding directly conflicts with the partnership moat you're building in Findings #6 and #7.

counterarguments.title: A 0.99 F1 score on 524 clips suggests severe overfitting to your specific dataset, not production robustness.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: The model was likely trained and validated on overlapping or similar data from the same 524 clips, creating a false confidence ceiling. When deployed on new courts, lighting conditions, camera angles, or player demographics, recall will likely collapse—especially for the 5 badges you're already missing. The 16 false positives might also spike under distribution shift, causing false badge awards that damage player trust.
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: DaaS badge prediction: F1=0.99 (precision=0.99, re

Court owners have invested in accounting, booking, and POS systems; the switching cost to Courtana's OS is massive. You'd need to either (a) build native integrations with 10+ legacy systems, or (b) convince owners to abandon their existing stack—both are $500k+ efforts for a <$1M TAM. counterarguments.title: Adding 5 visual descriptions to a prompt based on 21 total missed badges (out of 2065 correct) is not a finding—it's a hotfix that masks a deeper labeling or feature extraction problem.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: These 5 badges may be missing not because the prompt needs better examples, but because Gemini's vision encoder fundamentally cannot distinguish 'paddle tap celebration' from a normal celebration, or 'fresh fit' is too subjective to encode reliably. Prompt engineering will create false positives (over-detecting these badges) without addressing the root cause. You'll shift the precision-recall tradeoff without improving the model.
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: Prompt fix: 5 badges consistently missed — add pos

raw_data.target_title: Prompt fix: 5 badges consistently missed — add positive examples

counterarguments.title: Aggregating de-identified injury data from physical therapists creates massive regulatory and liability risk with near-zero evidence that courts or players will pay for injury prevention alerts.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: You're assuming physical therapists will (a) hand over injury data, (b) trust your movement-to-injury inference, and (c) that courts will act on your alerts. If you flag a movement as 'high-risk' and a player gets injured anyway, you're liable. HIPAA applies to any identifiable health data; 'de-identification' doesn't eliminate the clinics' risk in sharing. No evidence of willingness to participate or pricing model.
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: Injury Prevention Network

counterarguments.title: Brands already have warranty data and failure rates from their own supply chains—they don't need video clips to replace broken rackets, and paying for this 'insurance' cannibalizes their existing warranty margins.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: A brand like Paddle Tech already knows how often their rackets fail in real play via warranty claims and customer complaints. They have zero incentive to pay you a premium to detect failures on video if the outcome (replacement) is identical to their existing process. You're asking them to pay extra for data you'll own, while they absorb the replacement cost. No brand partner has signed on.
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: Brand Performance Insurance

counterarguments.title: Removing 'venue branding' detections is mathematically trivial and doesn't constitute a finding—you're just filtering your own data.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: This is not a business insight or a product discovery; it's a data quality step you should have done before reporting raw counts to anyone. If you've already been reporting inflated numbers to brand partners, you've damaged credibility. If you haven't, this 'finding' has zero impact. The real risk is that venue branding is actually *valuable* to some brands (Joola nets drive visibility), and removing it entirely undersells their true impact.
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: Net/venue branding inflates 7 brands — true share-

product_concept.name: BrandGap Filler
product_concept.first_screen: Dashboard showing 4 equipment categories (shoes, rackets, apparel, bags) with sponsorship void indicators. User selects a category → sees 5 missed badge examples from training data with side-by-side 'before detection' and 'after detection' annotations, then a 'Generate Sponsorship Pitch' button populated with untapped market size.
product_concept.why_powerful: Converts unspotted sponsorship whitespace into immediate pitch decks by fixing the detection blindness that's leaving money on the table.

product_concept.name: RealShare Court Analytics
product_concept.first_screen: Split-screen view: left side shows raw brand detection counts (inflated), right side shows 'corrected share-of-court' after filtering out net/venue branding noise. User toggles between 7 affected brands to see true vs. reported visibility %, then exports corrected sponsorship valuation report.
product_concept.why_powerful: Exposes the gap between what sponsors think they're getting and actual court presence, enabling accurate sponsorship pricing and realistic brand lift forecasts.

product_concept.name: PlayerClip Amplifier
product_concept.first_screen: List of 65 eligible players (5+ clips minimum) ranked by predicted viral score. User selects a player → sees their clip library with quality score (7.39 threshold highlight) and watchability index (6.78 benchmark), then one-click 'Create Viral Cut' that auto-generates highlight reel matching the formula.
product_concept.why_powerful: Systematically converts latent longitudinal player data into reproducible viral content by applying tested quality and watchability formulas instead of guessing.
